name: Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Electrostatic Potential
sections.body:
The electrostatic potential at a point is the work done by an external agent in bringing a unit positive test charge from infinity to that point against the electric field, without acceleration. $$V = \dfrac{W_{\infty \to P}}{q_0}$$ Unit: volt (V) = J/C. Potential is a scalar, simplifying calculations vs. fields.

For a point charge $q$ at distance $r$: $$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q}{r}$$ Potential is positive for positive source charge and negative for negative source charge. By superposition, $V_{\text{total}} = \sum V_i$ at the same point.

sections.heading: 2. Potential Difference and Field-Potential Relation
sections.body:
The potential difference between two points A and B is $V_A - V_B = -\int_B^A \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{l}$, the negative of the line integral of the field. It depends only on the endpoints (electrostatic field is conservative).

The electric field is the negative gradient of potential: $$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\dfrac{dV}{dr}\hat{r}$$ Field points from high to low potential. SI unit V/m equals N/C.

Useful corollary: in a region of constant potential, $\vec{E} = 0$.

sections.heading: 3. Potential due to a Dipole and a System of Charges
sections.body:
For an electric dipole of moment $p$, at point distance $r$ from centre at angle $\theta$ to dipole axis ($r \gg a$): $$V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{p\cos\theta}{r^2}$$ Special cases: axial point ($\theta = 0$): $V = kp/r^2$; equatorial ($\theta = 90°$): $V = 0$.

For a system of $n$ point charges, $V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{q_i}{r_i}$ where $r_i$ is the distance from $q_i$ to the field point.

sections.heading: 4. Equipotential Surfaces
sections.body:
An equipotential surface has the same potential at every point. Properties:

  • Work done in moving a charge along an equipotential is zero.
  • Electric field is always perpendicular to an equipotential surface.
  • Surfaces never intersect (potential is single-valued).
  • For a point charge, equipotentials are concentric spheres.
  • For a uniform field, equipotentials are parallel planes perpendicular to the field.
  • For a dipole, equipotentials are complicated; the equatorial plane is at $V = 0$.

Closer equipotentials indicate a stronger field.

sections.heading: 5. Potential Energy of a System of Charges and a Dipole
sections.body:
Potential energy of two point charges: $U = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}}$. For three charges, sum over all pairs: $U = k\left(\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r_{12}} + \dfrac{q_1 q_3}{r_{13}} + \dfrac{q_2 q_3}{r_{23}}\right)$.

For a dipole in external field $\vec{E}$: $U = -\vec{p}\cdot\vec{E} = -pE\cos\theta$. Stable equilibrium at $\theta = 0$ (energy $-pE$), unstable at $\theta = \pi$ (energy $+pE$).

For a single charge $q$ in external potential $V$: $U = qV$.

sections.heading: 6. Conductors in Electrostatic Equilibrium
sections.body:
Inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium:

  • $\vec{E} = 0$ everywhere inside (otherwise free electrons would accelerate).
  • Net charge resides only on the outer surface.
  • Just outside the surface, $\vec{E}$ is normal to the surface, magnitude $E = \sigma/\varepsilon_0$.
  • Entire conductor is an equipotential.
  • Charge density is higher at sharp points (basis of lightning rods).

Electrostatic shielding: a hollow conductor (Faraday cage) shields its interior from external fields, since field inside the cavity is zero regardless of external charges.

sections.heading: 7. Dielectrics and Polarisation
sections.body:
Dielectrics are insulators with no free charges. In an external field, molecules polarise — either aligning permanent dipoles (polar) or developing induced dipoles (non-polar). The polarisation $\vec{P}$ is the dipole moment per unit volume.

For a linear isotropic dielectric, $\vec{P} = \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \vec{E}$, where $\chi_e$ is the electric susceptibility. The dielectric constant $K = 1 + \chi_e$ (also called relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$). Inside the dielectric, $E$ is reduced by factor $K$: $E = E_0/K$.

sections.heading: 8. Capacitors and Capacitance
sections.body:
A capacitor stores charge on two conductors separated by an insulator. Capacitance is $$C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$$ where $Q$ is the charge on one plate and $V$ the potential difference. Unit: farad (F) = C/V. Practical units μF, nF, pF.

For a parallel-plate capacitor (plate area $A$, separation $d$, vacuum between): $$C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$ With a dielectric of constant $K$ filling the gap: $C = K C_0 = K\varepsilon_0 A/d$.

Capacitance depends only on geometry and the dielectric — not on $Q$ or $V$.

sections.heading: 9. Combinations of Capacitors
sections.body:
Series combination: same charge $Q$ on each, voltages add. $$\dfrac{1}{C_s} = \dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{C_2} + \cdots$$ Equivalent capacitance is less than the smallest.

Parallel combination: same voltage $V$, charges add. $$C_p = C_1 + C_2 + \cdots$$ Equivalent capacitance equals the sum.

Mnemonic: capacitors combine opposite to resistors.

sections.heading: 10. Energy Stored in a Capacitor
sections.body:
Work done in charging a capacitor is stored as electrostatic potential energy: $$U = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}QV = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C}$$

The energy density in the electric field is $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$ (J/m³), valid in vacuum. With a dielectric: $u = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 K E^2$. The energy is stored in the field itself, distributed throughout the volume between the plates.

Inserting a dielectric at constant $V$ increases stored energy (battery does extra work); at constant $Q$ (battery disconnected), energy decreases by factor $K$.

summary: Potential is work per unit positive charge; $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$. Equipotentials are perpendicular to field lines. Conductors are equipotentials with $E = 0$ inside. Capacitance $C = Q/V$ depends only on geometry; for parallel plates $C = K\varepsilon_0 A/d$. Series and parallel combine like reciprocals/sums respectively. Energy stored is $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$, with energy density $\tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$.
pitfalls: Potential and potential energy differ by a factor of charge: U = qV.
Potential is a scalar — add algebraically, not as vectors.
Inside a charged spherical shell, E = 0 but V is constant and equal to surface value (NOT zero).
Capacitors combine opposite to resistors (series in caps = parallel in resistors formula-wise).
When inserting a dielectric, energy change depends on whether battery is connected or disconnected.
Equipotential surfaces never intersect, and field lines are always perpendicular to them.
